summary: DeVault explores why client-side TLS certificates never caught on as an alternative to OAuth for API authentication. After reading OAuth2 RFCs while working on SourceHut's API, he sketches a hypothetical protocol where API clients use certificate authorities and CSRs instead of OAuth's token-based flow. He enumerates numerous advantages — stateless authentication, battle-tested cryptography, built-in revocation, and scope management through intermediate CAs — but acknowledges a nagging suspicion that he's missing something. The post is ultimately a thought experiment he puts to readers rather than a proposal he intends to implement.
key_points: OAuth is overly complex (100+ pages of RFCs), rarely implemented correctly, and involves no cryptography — yet it became the universal standard for API authorization
Client-side TLS certificates could provide stateless, cryptographically strong authentication with built-in scope management, expiration, and revocation via certificate hierarchies
Organizations could use intermediate CAs to limit each service's access scope, providing natural least-privilege enforcement
The traditional pain points of certificates (CRLs, OCSP, OpenSSL CLI complexity) are mitigated in centralized API contexts where you control all endpoints
Despite clear theoretical advantages, the fact that no one adopted this approach in decades suggests either hidden practical barriers or simply poor tooling and bad UX around certificates
money_quotes: The RFC is 76 pages long, the separate bearer token RFC (6750) is another 18, and no one has ever read either of them.
The process is complicated and everyone implements it themselves — a sure way to make mistakes in a security-critical component.
Have you ever met anyone who feels positively about OAuth?

TLS is basically the single most ironclad, battle-tested security mechanism on the internet, and mature implementations are available for every platform. Everyone implements OAuth themselves, and often poorly.
If you handle SSL termination with nginx, haproxy, etc, you can reject unauthorized requests before your application backend ever even sees them.
I have a nagging feeling that I'm missing something here. It doesn't seem right that such an obvious solution would have been left on the table, by everyone, for decades.
Web browser support for client-side certificates totally sucks, but that is a solvable problem.
key_insight: Client-side TLS certificates offer a theoretically superior alternative to OAuth for API authentication — cryptographically strong, stateless, and battle-tested — yet their universal neglect suggests that developer experience and tooling matter more than technical elegance.
